content.problemStatement:
You are given a positive integer NNN.

Find the number of arrays AAA of size NNN such that:


There exist two permutations†^\dag† PPP and QQQ, each of length NNN, satisfying Ai=max⁡(Pi,Qi)A_i = \max(P_i, Q_i)Ai​=max(Pi​,Qi​) for all 1≤i≤N1\le i \le N1≤i≤N.
Since the number might be huge, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

†^\dag†A permutation of length NNN consists of all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN −-− the size of AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the number of arrays AAA satisfying the condition, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Consider array A=[1],A = [1],A=[1], and permutations P=[1],Q=[1]P = [1], Q = [1]P=[1],Q=[1]. Here the array satisfies Ai=max⁡(Pi,Qi)A_i = \max(P_i, Q_i)Ai​=max(Pi​,Qi​) for all 1≤i≤N1\le i \le N1≤i≤N.
It can be shown that no other array satisfies the given conditions.
Test case 222: There exist three arrays satisfying the condition:

A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2]: Consider P=[1,2],Q=[1,2]P = [1, 2], Q = [1, 2]P=[1,2],Q=[1,2].
A=[2,2]A = [2, 2]A=[2,2]: Consider P=[1,2],Q=[2,1]P = [1, 2], Q = [2, 1]P=[1,2],Q=[2,1].
A=[2,1]A = [2, 1]A=[2,1]: Consider P=[2,1],Q=[2,1]P = [2, 1], Q = [2, 1]P=[2,1],Q=[2,1].

It can be shown that no other array satisfies the given conditions.
